data.title: Buffett Passes The Torch: What Greg Abel Means For Berkshire's Future
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Warren Buffett took the stage in Omaha this year and this wasn’t just another Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (NYSE:BRK.B) shareholder meeting. It was, in many ways, a farewell.

The 2025 meeting was packed with timeless lessons and one very important practical implication.

The torch is being passed down to Greg Abel, Buffett’s handpicked successor.

  • Who is Greg Abel?
  • How does this impact Berkshire’s direction?
  • And what should investors consider as we move forward from the Buffett era?

Ultimately, it’s important to consider why Greg Abel has been chosen, which can give us a clue as to what the company values in its CEO.

Buffett's Final Lessons

At the tender age of 94, Buffett continues to be sharp-minded and quick witted. The soon-to-be former CEO of Berkshire conducted the annual meeting in a packed auditorium, where thousands gathered to listen to his words of wisdom.

Let’s look at some of the key takeaways from the meeting.

You don't have to swing at every pitch. You just have to wait for your pitch.

Source: Warren Buffett.

Buffett reiterated his patient approach to life and investing. This is especially relevant today as Berkshire holds a record amount of cash.

We don't see screaming bargains.

Source: Warren Buffett

Even after the selloff, Buffett continues to suggest the stock market is at least fairly valued. Of course, we will know a lot more about Berkshire’s positions soon when the 13F is released.

Tim Cook has made more money for Berkshire than I have/

Source: Warren Buffett.

A lot has been speculated about Berkshire’s trimming of the Apple (AAPL) position. Buffett defended the company, and emphasized that this was done to raise cash and remain flexible. Apple does remain after all of Berkshire's top holding.

Lastly, Buffett spoke on tariffs too, the topic du jour:

The more prosperous the rest of the world becomes, it won’t be at our expense. The more prosperous we'll become—and the safer we'll feel, and your children will feel someday.

Source: Warren Buffett.

In line with what most CEOs have stated, Buffett said the tariffs were a big mistake. However, Berkshire is doing its best to mitigate this risk, by staying vertically integrated and production.

The Greg Abel Era Begins

The most important takeaway is the change in leadership that will take place at the end of the year.

Greg Abel will replace Buffett as CEO. Abel is already overseeing all the non-insurance parts of Berkshire, which accounts for two thirds of the business, so this seems like a natural transition.

Abel started his career as an accountant at PwC, and then later moved into the energy sector as he began to work for CalEnergy.

He meets people and they’re instant friends. He’s not loud or bombastic, but he’s 500% friendly. No one’s better at hosting a party than Greg. He makes sure everyone has a good time—not in an over-the-top way, but by making it personal.

Source: Wells Fargo Exec.

Abel is not loud or flamboyant, but he has been praised for his brilliance, candidness and being trusted. All very Buffett-worthy attributes.

Understandably, though, a lot of investors aren’t thrilled that Buffett is leaving. Not only has Buffett led Berkshire to consistent returns for decades, but his name carries a premium that few can replicate.

Berkshire’s stock has in fact sold off since the announcement, but not by a significant amount.

What to Expect From Berkshire

Berkshire faces a new set of challenges: rising rates, inflation, global fragmentation, and a scale that makes it hard to move the needle.

I talked in-depth about these dynamics in my last Berkshire post, where I explained how the company was positioning for a 1970s like environment.

I would argue that Abel’s appointment actually backs up some of these ideas.

The biggest defining characteristic that defines Abel, in my opinion, is his experience in the energy sector.

This is very interesting, because Berkshire has already been slowly accumulating large energy positions in companies like Occidental petroleum (OXY) and Chevron (CVX).

As the head of Berkshire Hathaway Energy, Abel has already been behind some key investments in renewable energy, grid modernization and some large-scale infrastructures.

With energy production and distribution likely becoming a significant bottleneck to AI expansion, Berkshire’s choice in CEO could turn out to be incredibly prescient.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, though, it's important to keep in mind that the value and philosophy that made Berkshire great will persist. Furthermore, Buffett will remain on the board of Berkshire, and any big moves that happen in the coming quarters will no doubt get the Buffett seal of approval.

I remain bullish on Berkshire, and I am very excited to see what the next 13F filing brings us.

Market Overview

US equities registered their first quarterly loss since the third quarter of 2023. Volatility surged and risk sentiment waned as markets grappled with considerable uncertainty surrounding the new administration’s policies on trade, immigration, fiscal spending, tax cuts, deregulation, and escalating tariffs. Against a turbulent market backdrop, technology indices fell sharply. Growth and small-cap stocks significantly underperformed their value and large-cap counterparts, respectively.

Global fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed government bonds as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff. The US dollar depreciated vs. most currencies following the US administration’s policies aimed at reducing the trade deficit.

Performance Summary

Hartford Balanced Income Fund (I Shares) outperformed its benchmark.

Security selection was the main driver of relative outperformance due to strong selection in industrials, healthcare, and consumer discretionary.

Sector allocation also contributed to relative results, primarily driven by our overweight positions in utilities and energy.

Our underweight allocation to investment-grade credit contributed over the quarter as spreads widened broadly. Our security selection in the sector also modestly contributed.

Our overweight to high-yield credit detracted over the quarter as spreads widened in this segment of the market.

Our security selection in emerging markets contributed. Despite credit-spread widening in the sector, the Fund was able to generate positive benchmark-relative returns in this sector.

Positioning & Outlook

Our universe remains rich with new opportunities. We continue to focus on finding high-quality businesses with strong balance sheets and sustainable dividends. We’re spending a lot of time on our downside stress-test scenarios and are confident in the sustainability of dividends and long-term value of holdings in the portfolio.

Within equities, utilities, energy, and healthcare represented our largest sector overweights relative to the benchmark at quarter-end. We were most underweight to financials, communication services, and industrials.

Within the fixed-income portion of the Fund, we maintain our underweight positioning in investment-grade credit as valuations remain relatively rich compared to history. We maintain a modest overweight to emerging markets as we find attractive relative value in this segment of the market.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

data.title: Fed To Hold Constant
data.description:
The Federal Reserve emphasized two things in the report it released following the FOMC meeting of today.

First: Uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further.

Second: The risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen.

The future is cloudier...and, the economy has moved into a state of stagflation.

Neither is desirable.

In terms of its primary monetary policy tool:

The FOMC "will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities."

And, to support the Federal Reserve goals of maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run, the FOMC "will maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25 to 4.50 percent."

In other words...the Federal Reserve will continue its monetary policy set for the foreseeable future.

Of particular interest to me is the Fed's assessment of economic activity: the Fed's assessment is that "economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace."

And, how does the Fed get to this position?

After all, in the first quarter of 2025, the economy declined!

But, the Federal Reserve discusses the first quarter in this way. From the fourth quarter of 2024 through the first quarter of 2025, the economy declined by 0.3 percent, or, by (the actual decline between the two periods) times 4.

That is the actual decline was the quarterly decline times 4: the actual decline was 0.075 percent...hardly anything.

(The year-over-year growth rate was a positive 2.0 percent!)

The item that pushed the first quarter into a decline was the growth of imports due to the changing tariffs being assessed. Imports are subtracted from exports and this gave the change a negative sign...but a very, very small one.

In other words, if one takes off the value of the decline in exports, the rest of the real GDP figure for the first quarter of 2025 is positive...and a pretty strong positive.

In other words, except for imports, the performance of real GDP in the first quarter of 2025 was a pretty good one.

As the Fed presents in its Press Release, "economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace."

"The unemployment rate has stabilized at a low level in recent months, and labor market conditions remain solid."

"Inflation remains somewhat elevated."

These descriptions could be applied to the state of the U.S. economy during the entire year of 2024.

The fear...as captured at the start of this article...greater and greater uncertainty combined with the possibility of growing stagflation.

And, this is the fear that the FOMC faces for 2025 and beyond.

For now, the Fed faces a relatively strong employment picture. It is the inflation future that appears to be the most threatening.

The Fed, right now, faces a major threat of losing the fight against inflation. As mentioned above, already there is fear that inflation is getting out of control again. And, one of the major consequences of the imposition of tariffs is the additional stimulus this pressure will further put on prices.

The Fed has fought for more than three years to bring U.S. inflation under control. It has done a very good job so far. It does not want to walk away from the battle quite yet.

The Federal Reserve will continue to fight to keep some sense of control over prices.

But, given the uncertainty that now exists in the marketplace, one cannot say that forces will not be released in the upcoming months that set off inflation again.

I think the Fed is going to do all it can to finish the battle against inflation it began over three years ago.

I believe that this is the message that the current press release of the Fed's Federal Open Market Committee is trying to get out.

Personally, I hope that the Fed and the FOMC can succeed.

data.title: Valuing Alphabet On A Sum Of The Parts Basis, Assuming Search Stops Growing
data.description:
Google Search Under Assault?

It seems that headlines constantly are talking about Google Search and how at risk the business is. Adding insult to injury, Apple’s Eddy Cue at Google’s antitrust trial testified that search queries on Apple phones fell for the first time in April.

He went on to say that chatbots from Perplexity or Anthropic could be replacements for Google Search on iPhones. We know that ChatGPT is also an option and all are generally cited as potential disrupters to Google’s enormously profitable search engine business.

Given the drubbing in Google stock, we thought it made sense to revisit the valuation on a sum of the parts basis. Many may or may not realize that Google is a tech platform with a variety of impressive assets, which we will break down and value separately.

The core business, Google Search, has seen growth rates fall on tough comps and on the law of large numbers. But even within Google searches, click-through rates have been in decline as many are finding answers within Google’s own AI summary boxes (in their Search Labs AI Overview at the top of Google searches).

Per the above, website publishers are seeing large declines in click-throughs, as Google searches are putting AI putting answers front and center. That somewhat obviates the need to click further.

And ChatGPT user growth has been phenomenal. While data is a bit conflicting, sources suggest that ChatGPT has between 400-800 million users (some weekly, some monthly). They hope to get to 1 billion users by the end of 2025.

On the plus side, GOOG launched Gemini 2.5 Pro last month. Reviews suggest that it is the best AI model out there.

Here was one take.

Source

Betting markets agree.

People seem to forget that Google was not the early mover in search. AskJeeves and Yahoo and a number of other search engines came first.

Personally, I use Copilot and Gemini and have little recent experience with ChatGPT (as I found it useless for financial analysis a while back). I still use basic searches daily.

Still, ChatGPT has the early mover advantage and while Gemini is growing, they only have 350 million users compared to 400-800 million at ChatGPT. And a lot of those are probably AI Labs results in their basic search, not necessarily dedicated nor many paying Gemini users.

Google doesn't exactly break this out for investors.

But what if we are wrong? What if Google Search gets really disrupted by Chatbots?

First of all, while Apple search queries might not have grown in April, it is unclear that that extends to all browsers. I recently added Google directly to my iPhone and have been searching on that instead of Safari.

Below is a chart illustrating Google search query growth over time.

In the most recent quarter, Google’s Search business grew revenue 10% year over year to $50.7 billion in revenue. Last year Search generated $198 billion in revenue out of $350 billion. That is 56.5% of revenue (or 50% excluding Traffic Acquisition Costs, TAC, which includes fees that Google pays to Apple to put its browser as the default).

So, definitely a concern if half their revenue is disrupted.

Below we see Search revenue (within Google Services).

Search market share is holding up well, despite ChatGPT’s being around for 2.5 years now.

Since October 2022, Google search share has fallen from 92.3% to 89.7%. If there were real disruption, shouldn’t this share be a lot lower? Or shouldn’t the number of queries be falling and revenue declining?

Anecdotally, I mostly use Google Search (and get annoyed when somehow Explorer turns Bing into my default search engine; instantly I can tell that search results are lower quality). The quality of results remains high.

While there is a lot of concern that chatbots will eliminate users googling, AI Search on Google has shown a recent jump in user satisfaction.

Search and Sum of the Parts

As we built a sum of the parts model, we dove into the numbers at Search. We observe that the Search segment within Services includes a LOT of stuff.

From the 10-K: “Google Services' core products and platforms include ads, Android, Chrome, devices, Gmail, Google Drive, Google Maps, Google Photos, Google Play, Search, and YouTube, with broad and growing adoption by users around the world.”

Within Services, Google breaks out Search revenue but not operating income, so we made some assumptions there. A lot of Google's products are free, like Gmail, Docs/Sheets, Maps, etc. Ads there do not seem at risk.

Outside of Search there is YouTube, Waymo, Google Cloud and a variety of Other Bets in other segments which we valued separately.

Recently, in a DOJ case, the court ruled that Google monopolized its ad technology (or tech for its auctions that brings together online advertisers as well as website publishers).

While above our pay grade, Google owns ad servers in the middle (and data for both publishers/advertisers). GOOG forces both to use their tech, and it is unclear exactly what the remedy is. The company points out that nobody is forcing these companies to do business with Google, and customers seem mostly happy.

So there is some breakup risk to Search/Google from this case.

We already talked about the antitrust case from last year for subscribers in some detail. From that case, there are decent odds of a ruling that Google is forced to divest Android or Chrome (where the company collects a TON of data for its advertising businesses).

But even in a divestiture, I am not sure why I would not use Google searches and Chrome. Would results really be that different? Would advertisers flee Google if they cannot provide browsing data? Search effectiveness would certainly be compromised.

But as Meta Platforms showed us, there are workarounds to track user behavior that I am sure Google is working on diligently. Chrome is free, and there could be a licensing agreement to pay for data in a separation or spin off.

In both court cases, Google will appeal until the end of time, meaning any material impact is probably years away (and will be negotiated).

Not surprisingly, Yahoo and ChatGPT have expressed interest in buying Chrome. The DuckDuckGo CEO said Chrome was worth $50 billion.

If they dismantle Chrome and search suffers, it won’t go away per se. Especially with all of the other valuable pieces within Search. I still think Google Maps is one of the greatest inventions from the 2000s, and advertising there could really become large.

Search may not be as effective for advertisers but still might prove far better than Bing for users.

And if Apple migrates to using Chatbots over search, would they be able to collect $20 billion a year in fees from say Perplexity to entirely replace Google? That is a lot of money to walk away from, and my sense is that Apple will want to offer Safari users AI and a high-quality search engine at the end of the day.

Downside Valuation / Search Disruption

To really stress Google's valuation, we put a 5x estimated EBITDA multiple on Search and assumed it stopped growing until the end of time.

For other segments, we assumed YouTube / Google Play subscriptions are worth $634 billion (with analysts suggesting YouTube alone is worth up to $575 billion).

In Cloud, we put a 18x EBITDA multiple on current EBITDA, where Microsoft is trading (but note that Google Cloud is growing faster than MSFT is overall and so is probably worth 20-25x).

Waymo is worth at least $75 billion, plus Google has $81 billion of cash.

Putting a $20 billion liability for courtroom drama/fines, and we peg the sum of the parts downside at $133 per share.

On 2026 estimates, assuming zero Search growth but continued growth in Cloud and YouTube, and we get a value of $143 per share.

That suggests 12% downside in a year and 5% downside on 2026 estimates.

The margin of safety is looking pretty good right now for those with a couple of years of patience. Big picture, Google is trading at 16x 2025 earnings estimates net of cash. That is about as low as it has ever been, and we can point to a lot of lower quality, far slower growth companies trading at 25-30x.

Again, $133-143 assumes a dismally low value for Search at 5x EBITDA and gives zero credit for Google’s Gemini business or its quantum computing technology.

In contrast, Meta Platforms (META) today, after its large decline, is trading at 12x forward EBITDA (and usually trades at 14.7x).

So, in our view, 5x is REALLY stupidly conservative.

Here are details on each segment with our assumptions.

Below are recent results.

Quarterly Results Refresher

These are Google’s quarterly results.

Apples to apples EPS grew 21% YoY. Cloud revenue grew 28%, Cloud margins almost doubled to 17% and YouTube ads grew 10%.

As mentioned, Search also grew 10%. Overall revenue ex-currency grew 14%.

Capex is expected to be $75 billion this year, with buybacks of $70 billion (3.5% of the float) planned.

Street estimates are $9.50 for 2025, but $8.75 excluding the SpaceX gain booked in Q1 2025.

2026 consensus estimates appear too high to us at $10.19.

In our upside case, we assume AI chatbots and search co-exist, that EPS grows at a 10% rate in 2026-2027 and EPS climbs to $10.50.

At 22x 2027 figures, an arguably conservative multiple given their collection of assets, and we see fair value of $232 (53% upside in 2–3 years).

We view recession risk and the law of large numbers as perhaps bigger concerns at Google. Already, some Asian direct sellers in the U.S. have stopped advertising on their platform (due to tariffs). Further economic weakness could put a dent in EPS growth this year. 2026 estimates will probably come down.

But so far, the numbers tell a story of continued growth with a lot of incredibly valuable businesses.

Strong buy, but tempered by recession concerns.

data.title: Trump Tariffs Trigger Collapse In China-To-U.S. Shipping Volumes
data.description:
If you’ve been inside a Walmart, Target, or Home Depot lately, you may not realize that a trade war is underway between the U.S. and China, the world’s two largest economies. Store shelves are well stocked, and prices have largely held steady.

All of that is set to change very soon if forecasts turn out to be correct.

Logistics firm Flexport reports that liners are slashing their China-to-U.S. carrying capacity at a faster rate than they did at the start of the pandemic.

Meanwhile, data provided by container ship tracker Vizion shows that bookings from China to the U.S. are dropping sharply due to President Donald Trump’s 145% levy on Chinese-imported goods. In the week ended April 21, liner vessels bound for U.S. West Coast ports were carrying only 90,831 TEUs (20-foot equivalent units), which is close to half the volume from a year earlier.

Gene Seroka, executive director of the Port of Los Angeles—the United States’ busiest port—told Bloomberg that “import-export with China is very limited right now.”

Economists Warn of Recession as Job Cuts Accelerate

Below is a near-term timeline of projected events, based on analysis from Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management. By mid-May, the first container ships will arrive at West Coast ports since President Trump announced “Liberation Day” tariffs on April 2. As previously noted, these ships will be carrying significantly less cargo than usual, resulting in empty shelves later this month.

Consequently, layoffs in the trucking and retail sectors are expected by late May or early June as shipping demand collapses. FreightWaves reports that job cuts have already begun, with up to 1,800 freight-related positions eliminated across Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Tennessee, and South Carolina.

Mr. Slok expects the U.S. economy to fall into recession this summer, and it’s worth mentioning here that real GDP contracted slightly in the first quarter, the first time it’s done so since the pandemic. A decline in Q1 was earlier projected by the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model, though a contraction of 2.4% was expected.

The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has slashed nearly 1% from its forecast for U.S. growth in 2025. The institution now believes the U.S. economy will advance only 1.8% this year.

Back-to-School and Christmas Inventory Face an Uncertain Future

Even once differences between the U.S. and China are ironed out—whenever that may be—it could take weeks for trade to normalize and goods to be fully restocked. Inventory for back-to-school and holiday sales is at risk, Flexport says.

The Toy Association, a U.S.-based trade group, is warning that Christmas 2025 could be in jeopardy. Nearly 80% of toys sold in the U.S. are manufactured in China. The Association notes that 96% of American toy companies are small or mid-sized businesses, and about half say they could go out of business in the coming weeks or months without immediate relief from the new tariffs.

Short-Term Pain May Lead to Long-Term Industrial Gains

The president and his advisors say that his protectionist trade policies will spur manufacturers to return to the U.S. His commerce secretary, Howard Lutnick, has described a future where U.S. citizens work in factories “for the rest of your life, and your kids work here, and your grandkids work here.”

Since Trump took office four months ago, a number of companies have announced plans to invest and expand their presence in the U.S. These include Apple, Honda, IBM, Merck, NVIDIA, and Taiwanese semiconductor maker TSMC.

My belief is that if Trump manages to bring these kinds of jobs back to the U.S. permanently, the short-term pain due to tariffs may be worth it in the long run. China joined the World Trade Organization (WTO) in December 2001, and since then, the U.S. has seen an overall decline in manufacturing jobs and output. Manufacturing as a percentage of the U.S. economy is now below 10%. That’s down from about a quarter of the economy in the 1950s.

What Should Investors Do?

It may not surprise you to learn that gold glitters as my safe haven of choice for investors concerned about economic and financial turmoil surrounding Trump’s trade policies. I’m clearly not the only one who feels this way.

Inflows into gold-backed ETFs have remained positive in each of the four months of the year so far. From January 1 to April 30, a net 6 million ounces of gold were added to these ETFs’ holdings. Meanwhile, the precious metal rallied 25.3% year-to-date through the end of April, marking the best first four months of the year since 2006, when it climbed 26.6%.

I believe there could be further upside potential as long as Trump’s tariffs remain in place, increasing investor demand for stability.

data.title: MarketAxess Holdings Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by MarketAxess Holdings Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: May Fed Meeting: Rates Steady On Policy Tightrope
data.description:
The Federal Reserve (Fed) has had to operate more as tightrope walkers than policymakers in recent months. Inflation that continues to remain above targeted levels has watered-down arguments in favor of lower rates. Slowing economic growth and the prospects of slower growth still from tariffs, on the other hand, have supported arguments to the contrary.

The focus of today’s Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting was not necessarily the rate decision itself, but rather the messaging in the policy statement and in Chairman Jerome Powell’s commentary regarding the potential path of interest rates in the second half of the calendar year.

To perhaps little surprise, the tone of the outlook remained largely on script, in the sense that there was consensus that economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace. However, details within the policy statement indicate that policymakers have flagged stagflation as one risk on the horizon. Here’s what else to know about the May FOMC meeting.

How Were Markets Performing Before The May FOMC Meeting?

Despite tariff uncertainty and growing alarm bells surrounding recession, equity markets have nonetheless performed strongly over the past several weeks. At the end of last week’s trading in the opening days of the May calendar month, the S&P 500 (SPY) logged its longest winning streak in over 20 years, with nine consecutive days of gains.

The rebound marked an incredible comeback following the White House’s tariff announcement at the beginning of April. The gains over the second half of the month, in fact, were enough to essentially recover all the losses since the announcement. Though the index is still in the red for the year, the recovery marks a notable vote of confidence from equity traders in the face of growing calls of an impending recession in the U.S.

Goldman Sachs (GS) had recently pegged the odds of a recession at just under the 50% mark. Similarly, in his annual letter to shareholders, JPMorgan’s (JPM) Chief Executive, Jamie Dimon, warned that tariffs would slow down growth, though he did not explicitly state that tariffs would result in a recession.

The commentary and predictions were corroborated to some degree by the release of the first quarter GDP report, which showed contractionary U.S. growth for the period. Traders, however, have continued staking their bets on a pullback in the tariff rhetoric. While the streak of gains was recently broken, the SPY, along with the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) and Nasdaq Composite (NDX), still remains higher for the month.

What Was Expected For The May FOMC Meeting?

There was a unanimous consensus prior to the May FOMC meeting that the Fed would hold pat on interest rates. According to the CME Fed Watch Tool, the odds were at 97%. The probabilities of holding steady were similarly better than half in June, with the prospects of a reduction not seen until July at the earliest.

The predictions were accurate, and this was not a surprise. Policymakers, most notably Chairman Jerome Powell, have been telegraphing their intentions in commentary leading up to the release. While Powell has acknowledged the tariff uncertainty and its impact on Fed policy, he had previously remained consistent in his messaging in stating that the Fed was in no rush to change course on the current trajectory.

Recent data has continued to support the “wait-and-see” approach. While GDP data has shown a slowdown in growth, the labor market has remained healthy, with job growth coming in ahead of expectations in April.

What Changed Following The May FOMC Meeting?

In the introductory paragraph, the May statement reflected on the swing in net exports and its impact on recent economic data, such as the GDP report. But the statement reiterated the past theme that "economic activity continues to expand at a solid pace."

More notable were the updates in the second paragraph of the statement. First, the FOMC stated that "uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further." This is likely in regard to the near-daily developments with tariffs from the White House and its potential impact on consumers and businesses.

The committee also then stated that the "risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen." This suggests that the committee may see the labor market weakening in the months ahead if the current trajectory of tariffs continues unabated. The fact that this was paired with a statement relating to higher inflation indicates that policymakers see stagflation as a real risk on the horizon.

Consensus remained among policymakers as the group held steady on the policy rate. Everyone who voted for the policy action in the last go-around also voted this month, with the exception of Neel Kashkari, who voted in favor as an alternate member.

Main Takeaways From The May FOMC Meeting

Consistent with the last several FOMC meetings, it was not the rate decision that was in focus, but rather the policy statement and subsequent commentary from Chairman Jerome Powell. Aside from the implicit nod to the risks relating to stagflation, today’s developments on both fronts weren’t necessarily groundbreaking. The meeting did, however, hint at how policymakers may adjust in the months ahead.

Quick-hitting and volatile tariff rhetoric out of the White House has complicated the equation for the Fed. With inflation still above the target rate range, policymakers have had to contemplate either holding steady on rates or increasing them if there was any indication that inflation would accelerate higher. The prospects of slower growth, however, have also created the dilemma that the Fed may need to reduce rates to counter weaker economic activity. But jumping the gun early on a rate reduction could add to short-term inflation pressures.

Seemingly, the Fed is in a lose-lose situation. The best course of action, it would seem, would be for the Fed to remain in a wait-and-see mode. Today’s developments in the policy statement and subsequent commentary indicated as much. In my view, I continue to see the Fed remaining in this position so long as inflation data continues to hold range bound and if payroll growth remains healthy. Overall, today’s FOMC meeting continued to reflect a patient and data-driven Fed in the face of an uncertain business environment.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Share Class Inception: A, Y - 12/31/97; F - 2/28/17; I - 2/27/09; R3, R4, R5 - 5/29/09; R6 - 11/7/14. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 12/31/97. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: Russell Mid Cap Growth Index measures the performance of the mid-cap growth segment of the US equity universe. It includes those Russell Midcap Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Prior to 12/31/24, the Fund's benchmark was the S&P MidCap 400 Index.

Top Ten Holdings (%)

Palantir Technologies, Inc. (PLTR)

4.62

Targa Resources Corp. (TRGP)

3.36

Tradeweb Markets, Inc. (TW)

2.79

O'Reilly Automotive, Inc. (ORLY)

2.73

2.51

DraftKings, Inc. (DKNG)

2.10

Deckers Outdoor Corp. (DECK)

2.07

Percentage Of Portfolio

27.08

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US equities registered their first quarterly loss since the third quarter of 2023. Volatility surged and risk sentiment waned as markets grappled with the implications and considerable uncertainty surrounding the new administration’s policies on trade, immigration, fiscal spending, tax cuts, and deregulation. Escalating tariffs and the prospect of additional levies in April worsened the outlook for inflation and economic growth, fueling fears of stagflation and recession. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) left interest rates unchanged as officials continued to monitor how aggressive trade policies could impact inflation, spending, and investment.

The Russell MidCap Growth Index returned -7.12% for the period. Within the Index, one out of 11 sectors rose for the period. Energy was the top-performing sector, while communication services, utilities, and consumer discretionary were the bottom-performing sectors for the quarter.

Performance Summary

The Hartford MidCap Fund (I Shares) underperformed the Russell MidCap Growth Index for the quarter.

Security selection was the primary detractor from relative performance during the quarter. Weak selection in information technology, communication services, and industrials was partially offset by our selection in financials.

Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock selection process, contributed to relative performance. Allocation effect was driven by our underweight to communication services and overweight to utilities but partially offset by our underweight to healthcare and overweight to consumer discretionary.

At the issuer level, the top-two relative detractors were our overweight position in Deckers Outdoor (DECK) and out-of-benchmark position in Marvell Technology, while the top-two relative contributors were our out-of-benchmark position in O’Reilly Automotive (ORLY) and overweight position in Tradeweb Markets.

Positioning & Outlook

Moving forward in 2025, we expect near-term earnings guidance across the market will be challenging. As such, we’re focusing idea generation on companies that have sold off as a result of high starting valuations or over-crowded trades. We believe the recent sell-off is presenting attractive prices for many high-quality mid-cap growth companies.

At the end of the period, our largest overweight was to consumer discretionary, and we were most underweight to healthcare and communications services.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

data.title: Woodside Energy: Louisiana LNG FID Cements Growth Trajectory
data.description:
It's been a while since I last wrote about Woodside Energy (NYSE:WDS), one of my favorite long-term investments in the energy space. With weak energy prices and tariff uncertainty, investors have punished the shares of energy companies, and Woodside has not been immune. WDS shares have lost 23% since July 2024 (Figure 1).

However, in my opinion, investors should use the pullback in WDS share price to accumulate the energy producer's shares, as I believe the company's future is bright.

Woodside recently approved the construction of its Louisiana LNG project, a $17.5 billion capex project that could add up to 27.6 million tpa in LNG exports to Woodside's portfolio.

Once the Louisiana and Scarborough LNG projects are completed near the end of the decade, Woodside is expected to see a doubling in LNG production and a step-up in operating cash flows. This should be enough to fund a significant increase in returns for Woodside shareholders.

Company Overview

Woodside Energy Group is Australia's largest independent E&P company with a global portfolio of assets, including offshore LNG projects in Australia, oil in the Gulf of Mexico, and most recently, the Louisiana LNG project in the United States (Figure 2).

Under the leadership of CEO Meg O'Neill, Woodside is positioning itself as one of the leading producers of liquefied natural gas (LNG), the key transition fuel for the global economy.

Louisiana LNG: A Transformational Project

In my last article, I mentioned that Woodside acquired Tellurian and the Louisiana LNG Project (formerly called the Driftwood LNG Project) in July 2024 by buying all of the outstanding shares of Tellurian for ~$900 million.

The Louisiana LNG Project is a fully permitted LNG export facility that is expected to produce 16.5 million tpa of LNG (3 trains), with expansion capacity for up to 27.6 million tpa of LNG (Figure 3). However, Tellurian lacked the financing and construction expertise to bring the project to fruition.

Enter Woodside Energy. In my opinion, Woodside's acquisition of Tellurian was a match made in heaven. Woodside is one of the few companies globally with the credibility and expertise to construct and operate complex LNG projects like the Louisiana LNG facility, as Woodside has been operating offshore LNG projects in Australia since the 1980s.

However, one of the key impediments to the Louisiana LNG being approved by Woodside's board was the lack of off-take partners. Due to repeated delays and cost overruns, several of Tellurian's original offtake partners, such as Gunvor, had canceled their contracts. Could Woodside find enough replacement partners for the project to advance it to a final investment decision (FID)?

Partners Lined Up; Project Sanctioned

The answer is a resounding yes. Over the past few months, Woodside has been able to secure off-take partners for the Louisiana LNG project, including agreements with China Resources for 0.6 million tpa beginning in 2027 and Uniper for 2.0 million tpa (1.0 million tpa from Louisiana LNG and up to 1.0 million tpa from Woodside's global portfolio).

In addition, Woodside has found investment partners like Stonepeak (a global infrastructure investment firm) to share in the capital costs (Stonepeak contributing 75% of the Louisiana LNG capex in 2025 and 2026, up to $5.7 billion, for 40% of the project).

Together with asset sales in other parts of Woodside's portfolio, such as a sell-down of 15.1% of the Scarborough JV to existing partner JERA (a Japanese utility), Woodside's management felt comfortable enough with the Louisiana LNG project to sanction its financial investment decision (FID) on April 29th, 2025.

Quarterly Results Become An Afterthought

With investor attention squarely focused on developments at the Louisiana LNG project, Woodside's quarterly results became an afterthought. On April 23rd, Woodside reported first-quarter results with revenues of $3.3 billion (+13% YoY) (Figure 4).

Quarterly production was 49.1 million BOE, down 4% QoQ due to the unplanned outages at Pluto LNG and inclement weather at the North West Shelf (NWS), offset by higher production at Shenzi and the addition of Sangomar.

Full-year guidance was maintained for 2025 at ~192 million BOE, with 28-35% exposure to natural gas hub exposures (Figure 5). Capital expenditures are expected to be elevated in 2025 due to the parallel developments at the Louisiana and Scarborough LNG projects.

Capex Elevated Over Next Few Years

With elevated capex, investors should not be looking for large shareholder returns in the coming quarters via the dividend. However, Woodside does have a long history of delivering shareholder returns with its dividend, so once the current development projects come online towards the end of the decade, patient investors should see a material increase in returns (Figure 6).

Woodside To Become An LNG Giant Within The Decade

Once the Louisiana LNG project and Woodside's other projects are operational towards the end of the decade, management expects the company to see a step-up increase in production and cash flows, with global LNG capacity expected to be ~24 million tpa and annual net operating cash flows of over $8 billion (Figure 7).

Compared to a current enterprise value of $33 billion, this implies nearly 24% operating cash flow yields, which could fund a large dividend increase or share buyback.

Oil majors like Chevron (CVX) and Exxon (XOM) trade at cash flow multiples above 8x (Figure 9).

Once Woodside gets through the current investment cycle, I expect the company's valuation should expand towards those of the majors like CVX and XOM. Assuming WDS trades at 8x P/CF and cash flows end up at $8 billion towards 2030, as management expects, we could see Woodside's equity be worth $64 billion, or 2.7x Woodside's current market cap of $24 billion. This would imply over $30/share for Woodside shareholders.

Could President Trump Mess Up Woodside's Plans?

In my opinion, the biggest risk and opportunity for Woodside Energy is President Trump's trade policies. On the positive side, President Trump wants to balance America's trade deficits. Since America has an abundance of natural gas and LNG (Figure 10), one easy solution is to ask foreign countries to buy more American LNG, which should benefit Woodside and the Louisiana LNG project.

On the other hand, if trade negotiations break down, foreign countries like China could boycott American LNG. This could be a big negative for Woodside. However, in my opinion, I believe rational heads will prevail in the long run, and America's comparative advantage in natural gas production should allow Woodside and the Louisiana LNG project to thrive. Furthermore, since LNG is a global market, even if China doesn't buy, other countries should still be able to absorb Woodside's cargoes.

Conclusion

In the short term, Woodside's share price has been negatively impacted by slowing global growth and lower energy prices. However, with the recent sanctioning of Woodside's Louisiana LNG project (formerly Driftwood), I believe the future looks bright for Woodside. By the end of the decade, Woodside should see a material step-up in LNG production and cash flows once the Scarborough and Louisiana LNG projects are completed. A fully ramped-up Woodside should be generating north of $8 billion in cash flows, which could translate into over $30/share in equity value.

I maintain my buy recommendation, but caution that the payoff for investors could take several years.

data.title: Fed's Wait And See Stance Could Persist Through To September
data.description:
By James Knightley, Padhraic Garvey, CFA, & Francesco Pesole

Fed leaves rates unchanged, but highlights elevated uncertainty

The Federal Reserve has left monetary policy unchanged with the Fed funds target rate range remaining at 4.25-4.50%. It was a unanimous decision with the accompanying press release stating that the economy continues to "expand at a solid pace", labour market conditions remain "solid" while inflation "remains somewhat elevated". All this phraseology is the same as last time.

The key modifications are that the Fed believes the "uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further” and that the “risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen". There is nothing particularly surprising in these comments with limited market reaction.

The 'wait and see' stance could continue for another couple of meetings

President Trump and Treasury Secretary Bessent will keep pressuring the Fed to cut interest rates, but those demands will continue to fall on deaf ears as officials try to gauge the inflationary impact from the Administration’s trade policies amidst ongoing labour market strength. Higher tariffs look set to lift prices while port operators and logistics firms are warning of a potential supply crunch that risks amplifying the near-term inflation threat. As such the Fed is in 'wait and see' mode, with Chair Jay Powell warning last month that “our obligation is to keep longer-term inflation expectations well anchored and to make certain that a one-time increase in the price level does not become an ongoing inflation problem”. This was repeated at today's press conference.

Consumer confidence readings point to the risk of a steep drop in spending

Fed cuts may come later, but end up being sharper

However, the scale of the slump in consumer and corporate sentiment to levels historically consistent with recession will be of concern to the Fed. Economic uncertainty and government spending curbs mean that trade deals and tax cuts need to be agreed quickly to prevent a stagflation infused downturn. Nonetheless, we expect that shelter-related disinflation, as already hinted at by the Cleveland Fed’s new tenant rent series, will give the Fed the room to respond with rate cuts later in the year. The market favours a July start point, but we see the risk for slippage and it may be that the Fed kicks things off with a 50bp cut in September, just as they did in 2024.

No material comment on the tapering process, and bonds trade both sides of the rate cut outlook

The main immediate impulse from the preliminary headlines was lower rates and a steeper curve, with most of the action coming in the real rates space. From that, there was a supposition that the Fed is primed to react to higher unemployment and hold its nose to rising price rise risks. That was quite the reaction, some brief and in fact balanced snippets from the press release. That type of price reaction needed to be validated from the press conference. In fact the commentary at the press conference was not quite in sync with the early price action, and market rates edged back up again. Overall, the net outcome is slightly lower for market rates, and in our opinion there is an avenue for that to be extrapolated ahead on a theory that we see some macro pressures build. The 10yr yield can easily get down to the 4% area in such a scenario. But for now, the contemporaneous economy is just about firm enough to validate market rates staying about where they are for now.

Nothing new on the bond roll-off programme (quantitative tightening). Last time, the Fed lowered the cap on the roll-off of Treasuries to $5bn per month, which is effectively zero. In turn that means that the Fed continues to be a net buyer of Treasuries, as the roll-off ranges from $20bn to $60bn per month. No change to this policy. At the same time a $35bn cap remains on the MBS roll-off, but we’ve not been hitting that cap, which means that MBS bonds that mature do not get re-invested. Anything in excess of $35bn would be re-invested in Treasuries (which is in fact rare). Long term, the Fed would have an ambition to take the MBS bonds off its books completely, and ideally replace them with Treasuries. No (new) commentary on this today though. Clearly all this being left to another occasion, perhaps awaiting less impactful times. For now the slower tapering process continues, and can do so as bank reserves remain ample enough. One technical help here comes from the debt ceiling, as until this is raised or suspended, the US Treasury is spending down and in so doing, adding to bank reserves.

FX not focused on the Fed for the moment

The dollar is slightly offered after the FOMC announcement, but the FX impact has been quite contained on the whole. Short-term rate differentials have had very limited spillover into USD-crosses of late, and the dollar was probably hit more by the Fed's concerns for a rise in both unemployment and inflation rather than any hawkish/dovish takeaways today. After all, the run-in to this FOMC meeting had seen a nearly 20 hawkish repricing in the USD OIS curve, but an underwhelming dollar rebound.

The dollar continues to embed a sizeable risk premium relative to its usual market drivers (rates and equity differentials, global risk sentiment). In EUR/USD that translates into around 4% overvaluation in our estimates, but the path to make markets comfortable with a substantially smaller risk premium isn't going to be smooth. A constant flow of positive news on trade risk de-escalation is necessary, but probably not sufficient in the face of the damage markets think tariffs are already inflicting on the US economy.

We think there will be sustained support around the 1.1250-1.130 area in EUR/USD in the near term: that has proven to be the region where most dip-buyers emerge. The balance of risks is still skewed to the upside for the pair.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post
